behaviors.description: Rows with missing required fields are rejected and routed to the dead-letter queue with original row numbers preserved.
behaviors.justification: To catch real DLQ routing failures, the test must interact with the actual DLQ mechanism and verify persistence. A mock that only records write calls would hide failures in message format, persistence, or connection issues. The behavior explicitly states rows must 'appear in' the DLQ—appearance requires real interaction.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test that reads a CSV file with rows containing missing required fields (e.g., missing email, missing timestamp), runs the full pipeline, and verifies: (1) invalid rows appear in the DLQ, (2) original row numbers are preserved, (3) original row data is intact in the queue.

behaviors.description: Date strings in multiple formats (ISO 8601, US MM/DD/YYYY, European DD/MM/YYYY) are correctly transformed to a single canonical format.
behaviors.justification: This is pure deterministic business logic with no I/O, external dependencies, or non-determinism. A parametrized unit test comprehensively covers the function boundary. No mock could hide a real failure here—if the transformation is wrong, the test will fail regardless of infrastructure.
behaviors.test_description: Unit test using @pytest.mark.parametrize with date input variants: ('2025-03-28' [ISO], '03/28/2025' [US], '28/03/2025' [EU], '28-03-2025' [EU alt]) and verify each transforms to canonical output format (e.g., ISO 8601). Include edge cases: leap years, single-digit days/months, year-only inputs.

behaviors.description: Processing a CSV file with 500,000 rows completes within the configured timeout and without running out of memory.
behaviors.justification: Memory exhaustion and timeout are runtime properties that only surface at realistic scale. A unit test with mocked data sources will not expose memory leaks, inefficient streaming, or actual timing behavior. The test must run the real pipeline against a large (or representative-sized) dataset to trigger real resource constraints.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test that generates or loads a CSV file with 500,000 rows, runs the full pipeline (read → validate → transform → write), measures execution time and memory usage, and asserts: (1) completion within configured timeout threshold, (2) no OOM errors, (3) all 500k rows processed (or rejected/routed correctly).

behaviors.description: When the S3 bucket returns a transient 503 error, the pipeline retries with exponential backoff rather than failing immediately.
behaviors.justification: While unit mocks can verify retry logic is triggered, they may miss how the actual S3 SDK (boto3) surfaces 503 errors, error codes in response headers, connection behavior, or how the real backoff library interacts with network timing. Integration testing with a realistic S3 mock (moto/localstack) catches SDK-specific failures that unit mocks hide.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test that uses moto (or localstack) to mock S3, configures the mock to return 503 on first call and succeed on second, executes the pipeline, and verifies: (1) retry occurs (not immediate failure), (2) exponential backoff timing between retries (e.g., 1s, 2s, 4s), (3) eventual success after retries.

behaviors.description: Rows successfully written to PostgreSQL are not duplicated if the pipeline is restarted mid-batch.
behaviors.justification: Idempotency and exactly-once semantics depend on real database constraints, transaction semantics, and state tracking across process boundaries. Mocking PostgreSQL hides duplicate-detection failures, lost checkpoints, or missing uniqueness constraints. Only a real database can verify that rows 1–N and rows (N+1)–total both complete without duplicates.
behaviors.test_description: Integration test that loads a CSV with 500,000 rows into a test PostgreSQL database, runs the pipeline to completion on rows 1–250,000, stops the pipeline mid-batch, restarts it to process rows 1–500,000, and verifies: (1) all 500,000 rows appear in the database, (2) no row is duplicated, (3) row uniqueness is enforced (via composite key or unique constraint).
